steps.text: Extract representative gas samples from an emission vent or stack.
Condition the sample gas to manage moisture, acid gas, and temperature.
Collect the gas sample in evacuated passivated silicon ceramic lined stainless-steel canisters, or equivalent.
Dilute the sample with dry ultra-high-purity nitrogen or air if VFC concentrations exceed 1000 µg/m3 (300 ppbv).

preconditions.text: If moisture is >= 3% v/v, sample gas temperature is >= 250 °F, or acid gases are present.
Field sampling and recovery staff must be trained in the best practices for extracting representative gas samples.
Source sampling teams should be trained and have experience with EPA test methods: Method 1, Method 2, Method 3, and Method 4.

constraints.text: Performance results apply only to target compounds listed in this method that have been determined to be stable when stored in canisters.
Use GC/MS as the basis for separation and quantitation of method target compounds.

steps.text: Establish safety procedures before using this method.
Become familiar with the safety recommendations in the gas analyzer's manual.
Refer to the safety data sheet (SDS) of each specific compound for safe handling instructions.

preconditions.text: Ensure sampling equipment is properly cleaned before use to avoid contamination.

constraints.text: Gas samples from stationary combustion sources contain significant quantities of CO2 and condensed water or water vapor which interfere with VFC compounds.
Water in the sample gas can coelute with analytes and affect response; manage water during preconcentration to prevent peak broadening and retention time shifts.
Acid gases and water condensed in canisters can result in corrosion of the interior surface of the canisters, compromising target compounds.
CO2 in the collected sample may coelute and/or interfere with the quantitation of target compounds.
If coeluting compounds have common ions, the common ions should not be used as the target's quantifying or qualifying ions.
Tripping, falling, electrical, and weather safety considerations must all be included in plans to collect source canister samples.
Perform leak checks of the sampling system, ensure proper exhaust ventilation, and handle gas cylinders safely to mitigate exposure to harmful compounds.
Exercise extreme care in working with high-pressure gas cylinders.
Avoid exposure to suspected carcinogens or other compounds with serious health risks.
Do not use butyl rubber or PFA materials for tubing and connections.
Minimize the use of fluoropolymers and fluoroelastomers for connections or seals to avoid adsorption and/or off-gassing of compounds of interest or introduction of other potential interferences.
Connection tubing for the dynamic dilution system must be constructed of chromatographic-grade stainless-steel (minimum type 316) or silicon-ceramic coated stainless-steel.
Mixing chambers or manifolds must be constructed of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic coated stainless-steel, borosilicate, or quartz glass.
Connection tubing for the static dilution system must be constructed of chromatographic-grade stainless-steel (minimum type 316).
Carrier gas lines supplying the GC must be constructed from clean stainless-steel or copper tubing with non-polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) thread sealants.
Flow controllers, cylinder regulators, or other pneumatic components fitted with rubber components are not suitable for carrier gas lines supplying the GC.

steps.text: Place the sampled canisters in their individual protective containers and then in a shipping box.
Store canisters in a dry location at ambient laboratory temperature.
Ship canisters to laboratory for analysis.
Measure and record the canister pressure upon receipt at the laboratory.
Compare the final pressure and associated sample volume (Eq. 19) with the theoretical volume sampled in the field from the sample time and orifice flow rate provided on the COC.
Setup, heat, and leak check the sampling system as described in Sections 8.5 and 8.6.
Connect and deliver a flow of UHP nitrogen or zero air to the inlet of the sample probe at a flow rate slightly exceeding that of the sampling system bypass flow rate.
Purge the system for at least two minutes.
Collect a 4 to 5 L sample in a 6 L canister as described in Section 8.6.
Submit the canister for analysis as a sample.
Perform the comparison of sample volumes required in Section 8.6.2.8 to check the field integrity of collected samples.
Flag data that differ between the two approaches by >=20% RPD, indicate if leakage is suspected, and invalidate the sample.

preconditions.text: Canisters are received at the laboratory
Data differ between the two approaches by >=20% RPD

constraints.text: Samples must be analyzed within 30 days.
You may not change the fundamental procedures of proportional sampling followed by GC/MS analysis.

steps.text: Visualization of chromatographic peak
Retention time
Integrated peak area
The top five spectral matches (as IUPAC compound) based on match factor (no minimum) with each match’s respective match factor and the name of the reference library the match comes from
The unknown spectrum from the sample that was searched
Measure the pressure of each canister upon receipt.
Compare the volume collected as received to the field post-test volume.
Flag the sample for potential exclusion from the data set.
Analyze a minimum of five calibration levels (ICAL).
Check for leaks, perform maintenance, and reanalyze.
Prepare fresh standards and reanalyze ICAL.
Flag any target analytes that are manually integrated in the calibration curve.

preconditions.text: If the volume as received is >5% RPD
Before sample analysis, annually, after a failing CCV, or when changes/maintenance to the instrument affect calibration response

steps.text: Assess calibration bias using variable volume injections from a 20 ppbv VFC calibration standard (200 ppbv CF4) with a single injection per calibration point.
Fit the calibration curve using a quadratic equation forced through 0.
Traverse Pt 1
Traverse Pt 2

Traverse Pt 2
Traverse Pt 3
Volume sampled calculated from intial and final canister pressures
Volume sampled calculated from orifice flow rate and sample duration
